reflection.quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
reflection.reading:
Monday in May. Nothing about it announces itself. The weekend is over, the week hasn't proven anything yet, and you're just here — making coffee, answering emails, walking past the same trees that were bare two months ago and are now showing off. This is the part of sobriety nobody warned me about: how much of it is just regular Mondays.

When I was 22, I would have called this boring. I needed every day to feel like something — a story, a problem, a recovery. Drinking gave me that, until it became the only story I had. What I couldn't see then is that the regular Monday is the prize. It means nothing is on fire. It means I'm not undoing yesterday or dreading tonight. It means I get to put one ordinary block on top of another, and that's what a life turns out to be made of.

Durant's line about Aristotle gets quoted to death, but it's true: you don't become someone in a single dramatic moment. You become them on Mondays. The version of me skiing in Whistler last winter, the one with friends who actually know him — he wasn't built on big days. He was built on a thousand quiet ones where I just kept showing up, made the coffee, did the next thing, and didn't drink.
reflection.meditation: What small, unremarkable action today is actually building the person you want to become?

affirmations: I am building a life on ordinary days, not dramatic ones.
Today I get to be present for whatever this Monday actually is.
I am capable of doing the next small thing without numbing it.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a Monday that doesn't require damage control. Grateful for coffee I can actually taste, for a phone I'm not afraid to check, for the quiet luxury of remembering the weekend. These were the things I thought sobriety would take from me — turns out they were waiting on the other side of it.
gratitude.closing: One ordinary day at a time.
